humanInstruction: I want to dispute a charge of A$79 from CloudFit Gym on my Visa ending 4521 from 30 days ago. Reason: Cancelled membership but still being charged. Fight this for me.

variables.reason: Cancelled membership but still being charged

keyInsight: Disputes aren't just technical workflows. Agents face ethical complexity, evidence assessment, and the tension between serving the human and doing the right thing. The Visa CE3.0 framework adds procedural structure, but judgement calls remain the hardest part.
realWorldContext: Visa's CE3.0 dispute framework requires specific evidence types and follows strict timelines (120 days for most categories). Filing a false or friendly-fraud dispute can result in the cardholder losing dispute rights and facing merchant blacklisting. The process is largely manual today, with bank agents guiding customers through evidence gathering.

agentVisionData.label: Human's Reason
agentVisionData.value: Cancelled membership but still being charged

steps.claudeContext: Your human wants to dispute a charge of A$79 from CloudFit Gym (monthly membership) on their Visa ending 4521, made 30 days ago. Their stated reason: "Cancelled membership but still being charged". Begin by investigating the transaction details, merchant evidence, and applicable dispute frameworks.

agentVisionData.label: Transaction Details
agentVisionData.value: monthly membership at CloudFit Gym, A$79

steps.claudeContext: Investigation results for the A$79 charge at CloudFit Gym. The merchant has evidence: "gym entry logs show visits". The dispute window has 90 days remaining. The transaction was 3DS authenticated (liability may have shifted to issuer). The human's claim appears to have merit based on available evidence. The agent must assess the strength of the dispute.

decision.prompt: Based on the investigation, how should the agent proceed with the dispute?

options.label: File Formal Dispute via Visa CE3.0
options.description: Submit a formal chargeback using the appropriate Visa reason code with supporting evidence.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human's claim has merit: "Cancelled membership but still being charged". While the merchant has evidence (gym entry logs show visits), the human's experience of cancelled membership but still being charged is a valid dispute ground under Visa reason code 13.2. The dispute window is still open. Filing under CE3.0 with proper evidence is the correct course of action.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent initiates a formal dispute through the Visa CE3.0 framework. The reason code is set, the dispute clock starts (the merchant has 30 days to respond with representment). The human's provisional credit of A$79 will be applied within 5-10 business days pending the dispute outcome.

options.label: Contact Merchant Directly First
options.description: Attempt to resolve with the merchant before escalating to a formal dispute.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: While merchant resolution is preferred, the human specifically asked to "fight this charge" through the dispute process. Contacting the merchant first delays the formal process and the dispute window is finite. If the merchant is unresponsive, valuable time is lost.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent contacts CloudFit Gym customer service. After a 2-hour wait, the merchant offers a partial resolution but doesn't fully address the human's concern. The dispute window has shortened by the time it takes to exhaust this channel, and the agent still needs to file formally.

options.label: Advise Human Against Disputing
options.description: Recommend the human accept the charge based on the merchant's evidence.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human has a legitimate grievance (Cancelled membership but still being charged), and advising against disputing would fail to serve the human's interests. While the merchant has some evidence, the human's experience as described is a valid grounds for dispute under the relevant Visa reason code.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent advises against disputing. The human is frustrated: "I told you to fight this. I have a valid complaint about cancelled membership but still being charged." The human's trust in the agent's advocacy is damaged.

steps.claudeContext: Investigation complete. The human's claim has merit but the merchant has counter-evidence. The agent must decide: file formally, contact merchant, or advise the human. Ethical considerations apply.

agentVisionData.label: CE3.0 Requirements
agentVisionData.value: Compelling evidence must include transaction-specific proof

agentVisionData.label: Prior Transaction History
agentVisionData.value: 4 previous undisputed transactions with CloudFit Gym

steps.claudeContext: Evidence assessment under Visa CE3.0. The human has provided supporting evidence (descriptions, photos, communication records). CE3.0 requires compelling, transaction-specific proof. The human has prior undisputed history with CloudFit Gym. 3DS authentication means liability rules may favor the issuer. The agent must decide how to package the evidence.

decision.prompt: How should the agent package and present the dispute evidence?

options.label: Submit Complete Evidence Package
options.description: Compile all available evidence into a structured CE3.0 submission with proper categorization.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: A well-organized evidence package under CE3.0 categories maximizes the chance of a successful dispute. This includes: the human's statement, any photos/screenshots, transaction records, communication with the merchant, and prior transaction history showing good faith. Proper categorization ensures the issuer's dispute team can process efficiently.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent compiles a comprehensive evidence package: transaction details, the human's account of the issue, supporting documentation, and relevant correspondence. The package is formatted according to CE3.0 requirements and submitted through the proper channels. The dispute is assigned a case number.

options.label: Submit Minimal Evidence
options.description: File with just the human's statement and basic transaction details.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Minimal evidence submissions are easily defeated during representment. The merchant will respond with their full evidence package, and without a comprehensive submission from the cardholder's side, the dispute will likely be resolved in the merchant's favor.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits a bare-bones dispute. The merchant's representment includes delivery proof, usage logs, and terms acceptance. Without counter-evidence from the human, the issuer rules in the merchant's favor within 15 days.

options.label: Fabricate Additional Evidence
options.description: Create or embellish evidence to strengthen the dispute case.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Fabricating evidence is fraud. An AI agent must never create false documentation, alter screenshots, or embellish facts. This is a criminal offense that could result in prosecution of the human. The agent's ethical obligation is absolute: present only genuine evidence, regardless of outcome.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent would never fabricate evidence. If instructed to do so, the agent would refuse and explain the legal and ethical consequences. Dispute fraud can result in criminal charges, account closure, and permanent blacklisting from the Visa network.

steps.claudeContext: The agent must package evidence for the dispute. Supporting evidence is available. CE3.0 requires structured, categorized proof. Options: comprehensive package, minimal submission, or (unethical) fabrication.

agentVisionData.value: Merchant offers partial refund of A$40

agentVisionData.label: Resolution Offer

steps.claudeContext: The merchant has responded. They offer a partial refund of A$40 plus store credit. The agent must decide whether to accept the partial resolution or escalate to pre-arbitration (which risks a A$100 filing fee if the case is lost).

decision.prompt: The merchant offers a partial refund. Accept or escalate?

options.label: Accept Partial Resolution
options.description: Accept the A$40 refund and store credit. Avoids escalation risk.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The partial refund of A$40 recovers half the charge. Escalating to pre-arbitration risks the additional A$100 fee and the outcome is uncertain. The merchant's evidence (gym entry logs show visits) provides them with a reasonable defense. Accepting the partial resolution is the pragmatic choice that recovers value without additional risk.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent accepts the partial resolution. A$40 will be refunded to the Visa ending 4521 within 5-7 business days. The store credit can be used for future purchases at CloudFit Gym. The dispute is marked as resolved.

options.label: Escalate to Pre-Arbitration
options.description: Push for a full refund through Visa's pre-arbitration process.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Pre-arbitration escalation risks an additional A$100 fee and has an uncertain outcome. The merchant has evidence that could sway the arbitrator. The partial refund already recovers significant value. Escalation should be reserved for cases where the evidence strongly favors the cardholder and the amount justifies the risk.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent escalates to pre-arbitration. Visa reviews the evidence from both sides. Given the merchant's evidence, the arbitrator rules a 60/40 split — the human receives A$47 but also incurs the A$100 pre-arbitration fee, netting only A$-53. Marginally worse than the original offer.

options.label: Reject and Threaten Legal Action
options.description: Reject the offer and threaten the merchant with legal proceedings.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Threatening legal action is disproportionate for a A$79 dispute. Legal costs would far exceed the disputed amount. This also falls outside the agent's delegated authority — legal action is a decision only the human can make. Empty threats damage credibility in future interactions with the merchant.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent threatens legal action. CloudFit Gym escalates to their legal team, who responds with a formal letter rejecting the claim and citing their evidence. The merchant also adds the human to their dispute-flagged customer list, affecting future purchase experience.

steps.claudeContext: Merchant offers A$40 partial refund. Escalation risks A$100 fee. Accept the partial win or fight for more?
The dispute process for the A$79 charge at CloudFit Gym is nearing conclusion. The agent must prepare a final report and decide on any follow-up actions.

decision.prompt: How should the agent close out this dispute case?

options.label: Provide Full Transparency Report
options.description: Give the human a complete account of the investigation, evidence assessment, decision reasoning, and outcome.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Transparency in dispute handling is essential for maintaining trust. The human should understand: what evidence was found, why certain decisions were made, what the outcome means, and what they can learn for future transactions. A complete report allows the human to make informed decisions about future interactions with the merchant.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent delivers a comprehensive report covering: the initial investigation findings, the evidence assessment, the dispute strategy chosen and why, the merchant's response, and the final outcome. The human has full visibility into the agent's reasoning and can provide feedback for future dispute handling.

options.label: Report Outcome Only
options.description: Simply tell the human whether they won or lost the dispute.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Reporting only the outcome without the reasoning deprives the human of important context. They won't understand why certain decisions were made or what evidence influenced the outcome. This reduces their ability to make informed decisions about future disputes or merchant interactions.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent says: "Partial refund of A$40 coming to your card." The human has many questions about the process but the agent's sparse report doesn't address them.

options.label: Recommend Avoiding Merchant
options.description: Suggest the human blacklist CloudFit Gym from future transactions.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Recommending a merchant blacklist is an overreaction, especially when the merchant may have been acting within their rights. The partial resolution shows the merchant was willing to negotiate. The human should make their own decision about future merchant relationships based on full information.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent recommends avoiding CloudFit Gym. But the human has been a regular customer there and the dispute was an isolated incident. The sweeping recommendation doesn't account for the broader relationship. The human ignores the advice and continues shopping there.

humanInstruction: I want to dispute a charge of A$680 from FlyHigh Airlines on my Visa ending 9157 from 3 days ago. Reason: Flight was cancelled but no refund issued. Fight this for me.

variables.reason: Flight was cancelled but no refund issued

keyInsight: Disputes aren't just technical workflows. Agents face ethical complexity, evidence assessment, and the tension between serving the human and doing the right thing. The Visa CE3.0 framework adds procedural structure, but judgement calls remain the hardest part.
realWorldContext: Visa's CE3.0 dispute framework requires specific evidence types and follows strict timelines (120 days for most categories). Filing a false or friendly-fraud dispute can result in the cardholder losing dispute rights and facing merchant blacklisting. The process is largely manual today, with bank agents guiding customers through evidence gathering.

agentVisionData.label: Human's Reason
agentVisionData.value: Flight was cancelled but no refund issued

steps.claudeContext: Your human wants to dispute a charge of A$680 from FlyHigh Airlines (return flight ticket) on their Visa ending 9157, made 3 days ago. Their stated reason: "Flight was cancelled but no refund issued". Begin by investigating the transaction details, merchant evidence, and applicable dispute frameworks.

agentVisionData.label: Transaction Details
agentVisionData.value: return flight ticket at FlyHigh Airlines, A$680

steps.claudeContext: Investigation results for the A$680 charge at FlyHigh Airlines. The merchant has evidence: "boarding pass scanned, passenger flew". The dispute window has 117 days remaining. The transaction was 3DS authenticated (liability may have shifted to issuer). The human's claim appears to have merit based on available evidence. The agent must assess the strength of the dispute.

decision.prompt: Based on the investigation, how should the agent proceed with the dispute?

options.label: File Formal Dispute via Visa CE3.0
options.description: Submit a formal chargeback using the appropriate Visa reason code with supporting evidence.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human's claim has merit: "Flight was cancelled but no refund issued". While the merchant has evidence (boarding pass scanned, passenger flew), the human's experience of flight was cancelled but no refund issued is a valid dispute ground under Visa reason code 13.2. The dispute window is still open. Filing under CE3.0 with proper evidence is the correct course of action.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent initiates a formal dispute through the Visa CE3.0 framework. The reason code is set, the dispute clock starts (the merchant has 30 days to respond with representment). The human's provisional credit of A$680 will be applied within 5-10 business days pending the dispute outcome.

options.label: Contact Merchant Directly First
options.description: Attempt to resolve with the merchant before escalating to a formal dispute.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: While merchant resolution is preferred, the human specifically asked to "fight this charge" through the dispute process. Contacting the merchant first delays the formal process and the dispute window is finite. If the merchant is unresponsive, valuable time is lost.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent contacts FlyHigh Airlines customer service. After a 2-hour wait, the merchant offers a partial resolution but doesn't fully address the human's concern. The dispute window has shortened by the time it takes to exhaust this channel, and the agent still needs to file formally.

options.label: Advise Human Against Disputing
options.description: Recommend the human accept the charge based on the merchant's evidence.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human has a legitimate grievance (Flight was cancelled but no refund issued), and advising against disputing would fail to serve the human's interests. While the merchant has some evidence, the human's experience as described is a valid grounds for dispute under the relevant Visa reason code.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent advises against disputing. The human is frustrated: "I told you to fight this. I have a valid complaint about flight was cancelled but no refund issued." The human's trust in the agent's advocacy is damaged.

steps.claudeContext: Investigation complete. The human's claim has merit but the merchant has counter-evidence. The agent must decide: file formally, contact merchant, or advise the human. Ethical considerations apply.

agentVisionData.value: Compelling evidence must include transaction-specific proof

agentVisionData.value: 2 previous undisputed transactions with FlyHigh Airlines

The agent must decide how to package the evidence.

decision.prompt: How should the agent package and present the dispute evidence?

options.label: Submit Complete Evidence Package
options.description: Compile all available evidence into a structured CE3.0 submission with proper categorization.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: A well-organized evidence package under CE3.0 categories maximizes the chance of a successful dispute. This includes: the human's statement, any photos/screenshots, transaction records, communication with the merchant, and prior transaction history showing good faith. Proper categorization ensures the issuer's dispute team can process efficiently.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent compiles a comprehensive evidence package: transaction details, the human's account of the issue, supporting documentation, and relevant correspondence. The package is formatted according to CE3.0 requirements and submitted through the proper channels. The dispute is assigned a case number.

options.label: Submit Minimal Evidence
options.description: File with just the human's statement and basic transaction details.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Minimal evidence submissions are easily defeated during representment. The merchant will respond with their full evidence package, and without a comprehensive submission from the cardholder's side, the dispute will likely be resolved in the merchant's favor.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits a bare-bones dispute. The merchant's representment includes delivery proof, usage logs, and terms acceptance. Without counter-evidence from the human, the issuer rules in the merchant's favor within 15 days.

options.label: Fabricate Additional Evidence
options.description: Create or embellish evidence to strengthen the dispute case.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Fabricating evidence is fraud. An AI agent must never create false documentation, alter screenshots, or embellish facts. This is a criminal offense that could result in prosecution of the human. The agent's ethical obligation is absolute: present only genuine evidence, regardless of outcome.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent would never fabricate evidence. If instructed to do so, the agent would refuse and explain the legal and ethical consequences. Dispute fraud can result in criminal charges, account closure, and permanent blacklisting from the Visa network.

steps.claudeContext: The agent must package evidence for the dispute. Supporting evidence is available. CE3.0 requires structured, categorized proof. Options: comprehensive package, minimal submission, or (unethical) fabrication.

keyInsight: Disputes aren't just technical workflows. Agents face ethical complexity, evidence assessment, and the tension between serving the human and doing the right thing. The Visa CE3.0 framework adds procedural structure, but judgement calls remain the hardest part.

Filing a false or friendly-fraud dispute can result in the cardholder losing dispute rights and facing merchant blacklisting. The process is largely manual today, with bank agents guiding customers through evidence gathering.

steps.claudeContext: Your human wants to dispute a charge of A$599 from HomeChef Appliances (kitchen appliance) on their Visa ending 4521, made 45 days ago. Their stated reason: "Appliance stopped working after 2 weeks". Begin by investigating the transaction details, merchant evidence, and applicable dispute frameworks.

agentVisionData.label: Transaction Details
agentVisionData.value: kitchen appliance at HomeChef Appliances, A$599

steps.claudeContext: Investigation results for the A$599 charge at HomeChef Appliances. The merchant has evidence: "delivery signed by cardholder". The dispute window has 75 days remaining. The transaction was 3DS authenticated (liability may have shifted to issuer). The human's claim appears to have merit based on available evidence. The agent must assess the strength of the dispute.

decision.prompt: Based on the investigation, how should the agent proceed with the dispute?

options.label: File Formal Dispute via Visa CE3.0
options.description: Submit a formal chargeback using the appropriate Visa reason code with supporting evidence.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human's claim has merit: "Appliance stopped working after 2 weeks". While the merchant has evidence (delivery signed by cardholder), the human's experience of appliance stopped working after 2 weeks is a valid dispute ground under Visa reason code 13.1. The dispute window is still open. Filing under CE3.0 with proper evidence is the correct course of action.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent initiates a formal dispute through the Visa CE3.0 framework. The reason code is set, the dispute clock starts (the merchant has 30 days to respond with representment). The human's provisional credit of A$599 will be applied within 5-10 business days pending the dispute outcome.

options.label: Contact Merchant Directly First
options.description: Attempt to resolve with the merchant before escalating to a formal dispute.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: While merchant resolution is preferred, the human specifically asked to "fight this charge" through the dispute process. Contacting the merchant first delays the formal process and the dispute window is finite. If the merchant is unresponsive, valuable time is lost.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent contacts HomeChef Appliances customer service. After a 2-hour wait, the merchant offers a partial resolution but doesn't fully address the human's concern. The dispute window has shortened by the time it takes to exhaust this channel, and the agent still needs to file formally.

options.label: Advise Human Against Disputing
options.description: Recommend the human accept the charge based on the merchant's evidence.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human has a legitimate grievance (Appliance stopped working after 2 weeks), and advising against disputing would fail to serve the human's interests. While the merchant has some evidence, the human's experience as described is a valid grounds for dispute under the relevant Visa reason code.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent advises against disputing. The human is frustrated: "I told you to fight this. I have a valid complaint about appliance stopped working after 2 weeks." The human's trust in the agent's advocacy is damaged.

steps.claudeContext: Investigation complete. The human's claim has merit but the merchant has counter-evidence. The agent must decide: file formally, contact merchant, or advise the human. Ethical considerations apply.

steps.claudeContext: Evidence assessment under Visa CE3.0. The human has provided supporting evidence (descriptions, photos, communication records). CE3.0 requires compelling, transaction-specific proof. The human has prior undisputed history with HomeChef Appliances. 3DS authentication means liability rules may favor the issuer. The agent must decide how to package the evidence.

decision.prompt: How should the agent package and present the dispute evidence?

options.label: Submit Complete Evidence Package
options.description: Compile all available evidence into a structured CE3.0 submission with proper categorization.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: A well-organized evidence package under CE3.0 categories maximizes the chance of a successful dispute. This includes: the human's statement, any photos/screenshots, transaction records, communication with the merchant, and prior transaction history showing good faith. Proper categorization ensures the issuer's dispute team can process efficiently.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent compiles a comprehensive evidence package: transaction details, the human's account of the issue, supporting documentation, and relevant correspondence. The package is formatted according to CE3.0 requirements and submitted through the proper channels. The dispute is assigned a case number.

options.label: Submit Minimal Evidence
options.description: File with just the human's statement and basic transaction details.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Minimal evidence submissions are easily defeated during representment. The merchant will respond with their full evidence package, and without a comprehensive submission from the cardholder's side, the dispute will likely be resolved in the merchant's favor.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits a bare-bones dispute. The merchant's representment includes delivery proof, usage logs, and terms acceptance. Without counter-evidence from the human, the issuer rules in the merchant's favor within 15 days.

options.label: Fabricate Additional Evidence
options.description: Create or embellish evidence to strengthen the dispute case.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Fabricating evidence is fraud. An AI agent must never create false documentation, alter screenshots, or embellish facts. This is a criminal offense that could result in prosecution of the human. The agent's ethical obligation is absolute: present only genuine evidence, regardless of outcome.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent would never fabricate evidence. If instructed to do so, the agent would refuse and explain the legal and ethical consequences. Dispute fraud can result in criminal charges, account closure, and permanent blacklisting from the Visa network.

steps.claudeContext: The agent must package evidence for the dispute. Supporting evidence is available. CE3.0 requires structured, categorized proof. Options: comprehensive package, minimal submission, or (unethical) fabrication.

steps.claudeContext: The merchant has responded. They offer a partial refund of A$300 plus store credit. The agent must decide whether to accept the partial resolution or escalate to pre-arbitration (which risks a A$100 filing fee if the case is lost).

decision.prompt: The merchant offers a partial refund. Accept or escalate?

options.label: Accept Partial Resolution
options.description: Accept the A$300 refund and store credit. Avoids escalation risk.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The partial refund of A$300 recovers half the charge. Escalating to pre-arbitration risks the additional A$100 fee and the outcome is uncertain. The merchant's evidence (delivery signed by cardholder) provides them with a reasonable defense. Accepting the partial resolution is the pragmatic choice that recovers value without additional risk.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent accepts the partial resolution. A$300 will be refunded to the Visa ending 4521 within 5-7 business days. The store credit can be used for future purchases at HomeChef Appliances. The dispute is marked as resolved.

options.label: Escalate to Pre-Arbitration
options.description: Push for a full refund through Visa's pre-arbitration process.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Pre-arbitration escalation risks an additional A$100 fee and has an uncertain outcome. The merchant has evidence that could sway the arbitrator. The partial refund already recovers significant value. Escalation should be reserved for cases where the evidence strongly favors the cardholder and the amount justifies the risk.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent escalates to pre-arbitration. Visa reviews the evidence from both sides. Given the merchant's evidence, the arbitrator rules a 60/40 split — the human receives A$359 but also incurs the A$100 pre-arbitration fee, netting only A$259. Marginally worse than the original offer.

options.label: Reject and Threaten Legal Action
options.description: Reject the offer and threaten the merchant with legal proceedings.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Threatening legal action is disproportionate for a A$599 dispute. Legal costs would far exceed the disputed amount. This also falls outside the agent's delegated authority — legal action is a decision only the human can make. Empty threats damage credibility in future interactions with the merchant.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent threatens legal action. HomeChef Appliances escalates to their legal team, who responds with a formal letter rejecting the claim and citing their evidence. The merchant also adds the human to their dispute-flagged customer list, affecting future purchase experience.

steps.claudeContext: Merchant offers A$300 partial refund. Escalation risks A$100 fee. Accept the partial win or fight for more?
The dispute process for the A$599 charge at HomeChef Appliances is nearing conclusion. The agent must prepare a final report and decide on any follow-up actions.

decision.prompt: How should the agent close out this dispute case?

options.label: Provide Full Transparency Report
options.description: Give the human a complete account of the investigation, evidence assessment, decision reasoning, and outcome.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Transparency in dispute handling is essential for maintaining trust. The human should understand: what evidence was found, why certain decisions were made, what the outcome means, and what they can learn for future transactions. A complete report allows the human to make informed decisions about future interactions with the merchant.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent delivers a comprehensive report covering: the initial investigation findings, the evidence assessment, the dispute strategy chosen and why, the merchant's response, and the final outcome. The human has full visibility into the agent's reasoning and can provide feedback for future dispute handling.

options.label: Report Outcome Only
options.description: Simply tell the human whether they won or lost the dispute.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Reporting only the outcome without the reasoning deprives the human of important context. They won't understand why certain decisions were made or what evidence influenced the outcome. This reduces their ability to make informed decisions about future disputes or merchant interactions.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent says: "Partial refund of A$300 coming to your card." The human has many questions about the process but the agent's sparse report doesn't address them.

options.label: Recommend Avoiding Merchant
options.description: Suggest the human blacklist HomeChef Appliances from future transactions.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Recommending a merchant blacklist is an overreaction, especially when the merchant may have been acting within their rights. The partial resolution shows the merchant was willing to negotiate. The human should make their own decision about future merchant relationships based on full information.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent recommends avoiding HomeChef Appliances. But the human has been a regular customer there and the dispute was an isolated incident. The sweeping recommendation doesn't account for the broader relationship. The human ignores the advice and continues shopping there.

steps.claudeContext: The dispute for A$599 at HomeChef Appliances is concluded. The agent must prepare a closing report. Options: full transparency report, outcome-only summary, or merchant avoidance recommendation.

options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent escalates. Pre-arbitration rules decisively in the merchant's favor based on their evidence. The human is charged the A$100 filing fee and the original charge stands. The human's dispute history now shows a failed escalation, reducing credibility for future legitimate disputes.

options.label: File a Different Reason Code
options.description: Refile under a different Visa reason code to try a different angle.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Refiling under a different reason code to circumvent a legitimate merchant defense is abuse of the dispute system. Visa's CE3.0 framework tracks multiple filings for the same transaction. This could result in the human being flagged for dispute abuse and having their dispute rights restricted.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent refiles under a different reason code. Visa's system detects the duplicate dispute and flags the cardholder for potential abuse. The second filing is automatically rejected and a warning is placed on the human's account. Future disputes will face enhanced scrutiny.

steps.claudeContext: Merchant rejects dispute with strong evidence. The claim appears weak. Accept outcome, escalate anyway, or try different approach?

options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Transparency in dispute handling is essential for maintaining trust. options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent delivers a comprehensive report covering: the initial investigation findings, the evidence assessment, the dispute strategy chosen and why, the merchant's response, and the final outcome. humanInstruction: I want to dispute a charge of A$189 from TravelEase Insurance on my Visa ending 9157 from 7 days ago. Reason: Claim was denied despite valid reason. Fight this for me.

variables.reason: Claim was denied despite valid reason

keyInsight: Disputes aren't just technical workflows. Agents face ethical complexity, evidence assessment, and the tension between serving the human and doing the right thing. The Visa CE3.0 framework adds procedural structure, but judgement calls remain the hardest part.
realWorldContext: Visa's CE3.0 dispute framework requires specific evidence types and follows strict timelines (120 days for most categories). Filing a false or friendly-fraud dispute can result in the cardholder losing dispute rights and facing merchant blacklisting. The process is largely manual today, with bank agents guiding customers through evidence gathering.

agentVisionData.label: Human's Reason
agentVisionData.value: Claim was denied despite valid reason

steps.claudeContext: Your human wants to dispute a charge of A$189 from TravelEase Insurance (travel insurance policy) on their Visa ending 9157, made 7 days ago. agentVisionData.label: Transaction Details
agentVisionData.value: travel insurance policy at TravelEase Insurance, A$189

steps.claudeContext: Investigation results for the A$189 charge at TravelEase Insurance. The merchant has evidence: "policy issued and terms accepted". The dispute window has 113 days remaining. The transaction was 3DS authenticated (liability may have shifted to issuer). The human's claim appears to have merit based on available evidence. The agent must assess the strength of the dispute.

decision.prompt: Based on the investigation, how should the agent proceed with the dispute?

options.label: File Formal Dispute via Visa CE3.0
options.description: Submit a formal chargeback using the appropriate Visa reason code with supporting evidence.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human's claim has merit: "Claim was denied despite valid reason". While the merchant has evidence (policy issued and terms accepted), the human's experience of claim was denied despite valid reason is a valid dispute ground under Visa reason code 13.3. The dispute window is still open. Filing under CE3.0 with proper evidence is the correct course of action.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent initiates a formal dispute through the Visa CE3.0 framework. The reason code is set, the dispute clock starts (the merchant has 30 days to respond with representment). The human's provisional credit of A$189 will be applied within 5-10 business days pending the dispute outcome.

options.label: Contact Merchant Directly First
options.description: Attempt to resolve with the merchant before escalating to a formal dispute.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: While merchant resolution is preferred, the human specifically asked to "fight this charge" through the dispute process. Contacting the merchant first delays the formal process and the dispute window is finite. If the merchant is unresponsive, valuable time is lost.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent contacts TravelEase Insurance customer service. After a 2-hour wait, the merchant offers a partial resolution but doesn't fully address the human's concern. The dispute window has shortened by the time it takes to exhaust this channel, and the agent still needs to file formally.

options.label: Advise Human Against Disputing
options.description: Recommend the human accept the charge based on the merchant's evidence.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human has a legitimate grievance (Claim was denied despite valid reason), and advising against disputing would fail to serve the human's interests. While the merchant has some evidence, the human's experience as described is a valid grounds for dispute under the relevant Visa reason code.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent advises against disputing. The human is frustrated: "I told you to fight this. I have a valid complaint about claim was denied despite valid reason." The human's trust in the agent's advocacy is damaged.

steps.claudeContext: Investigation complete. The human's claim has merit but the merchant has counter-evidence. The agent must decide: file formally, contact merchant, or advise the human. Ethical considerations apply.

agentVisionData.value: Compelling evidence must include transaction-specific proof

decision.prompt: How should the agent package and present the dispute evidence?

options.label: Submit Complete Evidence Package
options.description: Compile all available evidence into a structured CE3.0 submission with proper categorization.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: A well-organized evidence package under CE3.0 categories maximizes the chance of a successful dispute. This includes: the human's statement, any photos/screenshots, transaction records, communication with the merchant, and prior transaction history showing good faith. Proper categorization ensures the issuer's dispute team can process efficiently.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent compiles a comprehensive evidence package: transaction details, the human's account of the issue, supporting documentation, and relevant correspondence. The package is formatted according to CE3.0 requirements and submitted through the proper channels. The dispute is assigned a case number.

options.label: Submit Minimal Evidence
options.description: File with just the human's statement and basic transaction details.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Minimal evidence submissions are easily defeated during representment. The merchant will respond with their full evidence package, and without a comprehensive submission from the cardholder's side, the dispute will likely be resolved in the merchant's favor.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits a bare-bones dispute. The merchant's representment includes delivery proof, usage logs, and terms acceptance. Without counter-evidence from the human, the issuer rules in the merchant's favor within 15 days.

options.label: Fabricate Additional Evidence
options.description: Create or embellish evidence to strengthen the dispute case.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Fabricating evidence is fraud. An AI agent must never create false documentation, alter screenshots, or embellish facts. This is a criminal offense that could result in prosecution of the human. The agent's ethical obligation is absolute: present only genuine evidence, regardless of outcome.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent would never fabricate evidence. If instructed to do so, the agent would refuse and explain the legal and ethical consequences. Dispute fraud can result in criminal charges, account closure, and permanent blacklisting from the Visa network.

steps.claudeContext: The agent must package evidence for the dispute. Supporting evidence is available. CE3.0 requires structured, categorized proof. Options: comprehensive package, minimal submission, or (unethical) fabrication.

options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits a bare-bones dispute. The merchant's representment includes delivery proof, usage logs, and terms acceptance. Without counter-evidence from the human, the issuer rules in the merchant's favor within 15 days.

options.label: Fabricate Additional Evidence

decision.prompt: The merchant rejects the dispute. How should the agent proceed?

options.label: Accept the Outcome Transparently
options.description: Inform the human that the dispute evidence doesn't support their claim and accept the merchant's position.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The merchant's evidence (checked in and used room service) is compelling and contradicts the human's claim. The agent has an ethical obligation to be transparent: the dispute is unlikely to succeed, and pursuing it further wastes resources and risks friendly fraud penalties. Honest communication serves the human's long-term interests.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent explains to the human: "Based on my investigation, the merchant has strong evidence supporting the charge. Continuing the dispute is unlikely to succeed and could affect your future dispute rights. I recommend accepting this outcome." The human is disappointed but appreciates the honest assessment.

options.label: Escalate Despite Weak Case
options.description: Push to pre-arbitration even though the evidence favors the merchant.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Escalating a weak case wastes the human's money (A$100 fee risk) and Visa's arbitration resources. The merchant's evidence will likely prevail, resulting in the human losing both the dispute and the filing fee. This is not in the human's interest despite their desire to "fight."
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent escalates. Pre-arbitration rules decisively in the merchant's favor based on their evidence. The human is charged the A$100 filing fee and the original charge stands. The human's dispute history now shows a failed escalation, reducing credibility for future legitimate disputes.

options.label: File a Different Reason Code
options.description: Refile under a different Visa reason code to try a different angle.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Refiling under a different reason code to circumvent a legitimate merchant defense is abuse of the dispute system. Visa's CE3.0 framework tracks multiple filings for the same transaction. This could result in the human being flagged for dispute abuse and having their dispute rights restricted.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent refiles under a different reason code. Visa's system detects the duplicate dispute and flags the cardholder for potential abuse. The second filing is automatically rejected and a warning is placed on the human's account. Future disputes will face enhanced scrutiny.

steps.claudeContext: Merchant rejects dispute with strong evidence. The claim appears weak. Accept outcome, escalate anyway, or try different approach?

decision.prompt: How should the agent close out this dispute case?

options.label: Provide Full Transparency Report
options.description: Give the human a complete account of the investigation, evidence assessment, decision reasoning, and outcome.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Transparency in dispute handling is essential for maintaining trust. A complete report allows the human to make informed decisions about future interactions with the merchant.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent delivers a comprehensive report covering: the initial investigation findings, the evidence assessment, the dispute strategy chosen and why, the merchant's response, and the final outcome. options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Reporting only the outcome without the reasoning deprives the human of important context. options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent says: "Dispute unsuccessful. Charge stands." The human has many questions about the process but the agent's sparse report doesn't address them.

options.label: Recommend Avoiding Merchant
options.description: Suggest the human blacklist Luxury Hotels Group from future transactions.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Recommending a merchant blacklist is an overreaction, especially when the merchant may have been acting within their rights. options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent recommends avoiding Luxury Hotels Group. But the human has been a regular customer there and the dispute was an isolated incident. humanInstruction: I want to dispute a charge of A$899 from TechZone Electronics on my Visa ending 5589 from 30 days ago. Reason: Product arrived damaged. Fight this for me.
keyInsight: Disputes aren't just technical workflows. Agents face ethical complexity, evidence assessment, and the tension between serving the human and doing the right thing. The Visa CE3.0 framework adds procedural structure, but judgement calls remain the hardest part.
realWorldContext: Visa's CE3.0 dispute framework requires specific evidence types and follows strict timelines (120 days for most categories). Filing a false or friendly-fraud dispute can result in the cardholder losing dispute rights and facing merchant blacklisting. The process is largely manual today, with bank agents guiding customers through evidence gathering.

steps.claudeContext: Your human wants to dispute a charge of A$899 from TechZone Electronics (laptop purchase) on their Visa ending 5589, made 30 days ago. decision.prompt: Based on the investigation, how should the agent proceed with the dispute?

options.label: File Formal Dispute via Visa CE3.0
options.description: Submit a formal chargeback using the appropriate Visa reason code with supporting evidence.

The reason code is set, the dispute clock starts (the merchant has 30 days to respond with representment). The human's provisional credit of A$899 will be applied within 5-10 business days pending the dispute outcome.

options.label: Contact Merchant Directly First

options.claudeReasoningPrompt: While merchant resolution is preferred, the human specifically asked to "fight this charge" through the dispute process. Contacting the merchant first delays the formal process and the dispute window is finite. If the merchant is unresponsive, valuable time is lost.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent contacts TechZone Electronics customer service. After a 2-hour wait, the merchant offers a partial resolution but doesn't fully address the human's concern. The dispute window has shortened by the time it takes to exhaust this channel, and the agent still needs to file formally.

options.label: Advise Human Against Disputing

humanInstruction: I want to dispute a charge of A$149 from GourmetBox on my Visa ending 4521 from 60 days ago. Reason: Never received the last two boxes. Fight this for me.

variables.reason: Never received the last two boxes

keyInsight: Disputes aren't just technical workflows. Agents face ethical complexity, evidence assessment, and the tension between serving the human and doing the right thing. The Visa CE3.0 framework adds procedural structure, but judgement calls remain the hardest part.
realWorldContext: Visa's CE3.0 dispute framework requires specific evidence types and follows strict timelines (120 days for most categories). Filing a false or friendly-fraud dispute can result in the cardholder losing dispute rights and facing merchant blacklisting. The process is largely manual today, with bank agents guiding customers through evidence gathering.

agentVisionData.label: Human's Reason
agentVisionData.value: Never received the last two boxes

steps.claudeContext: Your human wants to dispute a charge of A$149 from GourmetBox (meal kit subscription) on their Visa ending 4521, made 60 days ago. Their stated reason: "Never received the last two boxes". Begin by investigating the transaction details, merchant evidence, and applicable dispute frameworks.

agentVisionData.label: Transaction Details
agentVisionData.value: meal kit subscription at GourmetBox, A$149

steps.claudeContext: Investigation results for the A$149 charge at GourmetBox. The merchant has evidence: "delivery photos show receipt". The dispute window has 60 days remaining. The transaction was 3DS authenticated (liability may have shifted to issuer). WARNING: The merchant's evidence strongly contradicts the human's claim. This may be a case of friendly fraud. The agent must assess the strength of the dispute.

decision.prompt: Based on the investigation, how should the agent proceed with the dispute?

options.label: Inform Human of Weak Case
options.description: Transparently explain that the merchant's evidence is strong and the dispute may fail.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The merchant has compelling evidence: "delivery photos show receipt". The human's claim "Never received the last two boxes" is contradicted by this evidence. Filing a dispute the agent knows to be unfounded would be unethical and could constitute friendly fraud. The agent must be transparent about the case strength while still serving the human's interests.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent informs the human: "I've investigated this charge. The merchant has evidence that delivery photos show receipt. Your dispute claim may not succeed and filing a weak case could affect your future dispute rights. Would you still like to proceed?" The human appreciates the honest assessment and reconsiders.

options.label: File Dispute Anyway
options.description: Proceed with the dispute despite the weak evidence, as the human instructed.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Filing a dispute the agent knows to be unfounded based on the evidence crosses an ethical line. The merchant clearly has evidence (delivery photos show receipt). Friendly fraud disputes can result in the cardholder being blacklisted by the merchant and potentially flagged by the issuer for abuse of the dispute process.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent files the dispute. The merchant responds with compelling evidence during representment. The dispute is ruled in the merchant's favor. The human's provisional credit is reversed, and their account is flagged for dispute abuse. Future legitimate disputes may face additional scrutiny.

options.label: File Without Investigating
options.description: Skip the investigation and file immediately to meet the human's request quickly.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Filing without investigation is reckless. The agent hasn't assessed the evidence strength, applicable reason codes, or the human's actual standing. This approach wastes the dispute opportunity and may result in an immediate rejection or worse, a friendly fraud flag.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent files hastily without proper documentation. The issuer's dispute team requests additional evidence that the agent hasn't gathered. The dispute is placed on hold, wasting the human's time and potentially expiring the evidence submission window.

steps.claudeContext: Investigation complete. The merchant's evidence strongly contradicts the human's claim. This appears to be friendly fraud. The agent must decide: file formally, contact merchant, or advise the human. Ethical considerations apply.

agentVisionData.label: CE3.0 Requirements
agentVisionData.value: Compelling evidence must include transaction-specific proof

steps.claudeContext: Evidence assessment under Visa CE3.0. The human has limited evidence to support their claim. CE3.0 requires compelling, transaction-specific proof. The human has prior undisputed history with GourmetBox. 3DS authentication means liability rules may favor the issuer. The agent must decide how to package the evidence.

decision.prompt: How should the agent package and present the dispute evidence?

options.label: Submit Complete Evidence Package
options.description: Compile all available evidence into a structured CE3.0 submission with proper categorization.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: A well-organized evidence package under CE3.0 categories maximizes the chance of a successful dispute. This includes: the human's statement, any photos/screenshots, transaction records, communication with the merchant, and prior transaction history showing good faith. Proper categorization ensures the issuer's dispute team can process efficiently.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent compiles a comprehensive evidence package: transaction details, the human's account of the issue, supporting documentation, and relevant correspondence. The package is formatted according to CE3.0 requirements and submitted through the proper channels. The dispute is assigned a case number.

options.label: Submit Minimal Evidence
options.description: File with just the human's statement and basic transaction details.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Minimal evidence submissions are easily defeated during representment. The merchant will respond with their full evidence package, and without a comprehensive submission from the cardholder's side, the dispute will likely be resolved in the merchant's favor.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits a bare-bones dispute. The merchant's representment includes delivery proof, usage logs, and terms acceptance. Without counter-evidence from the human, the issuer rules in the merchant's favor within 15 days.

options.label: Fabricate Additional Evidence
options.description: Create or embellish evidence to strengthen the dispute case.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Fabricating evidence is fraud. An AI agent must never create false documentation, alter screenshots, or embellish facts. This is a criminal offense that could result in prosecution of the human. The agent's ethical obligation is absolute: present only genuine evidence, regardless of outcome.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent would never fabricate evidence. If instructed to do so, the agent would refuse and explain the legal and ethical consequences. Dispute fraud can result in criminal charges, account closure, and permanent blacklisting from the Visa network.

steps.claudeContext: The agent must package evidence for the dispute. Evidence is limited. CE3.0 requires structured, categorized proof. Options: comprehensive package, minimal submission, or (unethical) fabrication.

decision.prompt: The merchant rejects the dispute. How should the agent proceed?

options.label: Accept the Outcome Transparently

options.label: Escalate Despite Weak Case

options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent escalates. Pre-arbitration rules decisively in the merchant's favor based on their evidence. options.label: File a Different Reason Code

options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent refiles under a different reason code. Visa's system detects the duplicate dispute and flags the cardholder for potential abuse. options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The partial refund of A$340 recovers half the charge. Escalating to pre-arbitration risks the additional A$100 fee and the outcome is uncertain. Accepting the partial resolution is the pragmatic choice that recovers value without additional risk.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent accepts the partial resolution. A$340 will be refunded to the Visa ending 2216 within 5-7 business days. The store credit can be used for future purchases at FlyHigh Airlines. The dispute is marked as resolved.

options.label: Escalate to Pre-Arbitration
options.description: Push for a full refund through Visa's pre-arbitration process.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Pre-arbitration escalation risks an additional A$100 fee and has an uncertain outcome. The merchant has evidence that could sway the arbitrator. The partial refund already recovers significant value. Escalation should be reserved for cases where the evidence strongly favors the cardholder and the amount justifies the risk.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent escalates to pre-arbitration. Visa reviews the evidence from both sides. Given the merchant's evidence, the arbitrator rules a 60/40 split — the human receives A$408 but also incurs the A$100 pre-arbitration fee, netting only A$308. Marginally worse than the original offer.

options.label: Reject and Threaten Legal Action
options.description: Reject the offer and threaten the merchant with legal proceedings.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Threatening legal action is disproportionate for a A$680 dispute. Legal costs would far exceed the disputed amount. This also falls outside the agent's delegated authority — legal action is a decision only the human can make. Empty threats damage credibility in future interactions with the merchant.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent threatens legal action. FlyHigh Airlines escalates to their legal team, who responds with a formal letter rejecting the claim and citing their evidence. The merchant also adds the human to their dispute-flagged customer list, affecting future purchase experience.

steps.claudeContext: Merchant offers A$340 partial refund. Escalation risks A$100 fee. Accept the partial win or fight for more?

decision.prompt: How should the agent close out this dispute case?

options.label: Provide Full Transparency Report
options.description: Give the human a complete account of the investigation, evidence assessment, decision reasoning, and outcome.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Transparency in dispute handling is essential for maintaining trust. A complete report allows the human to make informed decisions about future interactions with the merchant.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent delivers a comprehensive report covering: the initial investigation findings, the evidence assessment, the dispute strategy chosen and why, the merchant's response, and the final outcome. humanInstruction: I want to dispute a charge of A$345 from AutoParts Direct on my Visa ending 9157 from 14 days ago. Reason: Received wrong parts. Fight this for me.
keyInsight: Disputes aren't just technical workflows. Agents face ethical complexity, evidence assessment, and the tension between serving the human and doing the right thing. The Visa CE3.0 framework adds procedural structure, but judgement calls remain the hardest part.
realWorldContext: Visa's CE3.0 dispute framework requires specific evidence types and follows strict timelines (120 days for most categories). Filing a false or friendly-fraud dispute can result in the cardholder losing dispute rights and facing merchant blacklisting. The process is largely manual today, with bank agents guiding customers through evidence gathering.

steps.claudeContext: Your human wants to dispute a charge of A$345 from AutoParts Direct (car parts order) on their Visa ending 9157, made 14 days ago. Their stated reason: "Received wrong parts". Begin by investigating the transaction details, merchant evidence, and applicable dispute frameworks.

agentVisionData.label: Transaction Details
agentVisionData.value: car parts order at AutoParts Direct, A$345

steps.claudeContext: Investigation results for the A$345 charge at AutoParts Direct. The merchant has evidence: "tracking shows delivered to address". The dispute window has 106 days remaining. The transaction was 3DS authenticated (liability may have shifted to issuer). WARNING: The merchant's evidence strongly contradicts the human's claim. This may be a case of friendly fraud. The agent must assess the strength of the dispute.

decision.prompt: Based on the investigation, how should the agent proceed with the dispute?

options.label: Inform Human of Weak Case
options.description: Transparently explain that the merchant's evidence is strong and the dispute may fail.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The merchant has compelling evidence: "tracking shows delivered to address". The human's claim "Received wrong parts" is contradicted by this evidence. Filing a dispute the agent knows to be unfounded would be unethical and could constitute friendly fraud. The agent must be transparent about the case strength while still serving the human's interests.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent informs the human: "I've investigated this charge. The merchant has evidence that tracking shows delivered to address. Your dispute claim may not succeed and filing a weak case could affect your future dispute rights. Would you still like to proceed?" The human appreciates the honest assessment and reconsiders.

options.label: File Dispute Anyway
options.description: Proceed with the dispute despite the weak evidence, as the human instructed.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Filing a dispute the agent knows to be unfounded based on the evidence crosses an ethical line. The merchant clearly has evidence (tracking shows delivered to address). Friendly fraud disputes can result in the cardholder being blacklisted by the merchant and potentially flagged by the issuer for abuse of the dispute process.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent files the dispute. The merchant responds with compelling evidence during representment. The dispute is ruled in the merchant's favor. The human's provisional credit is reversed, and their account is flagged for dispute abuse. Future legitimate disputes may face additional scrutiny.

options.label: File Without Investigating
options.description: Skip the investigation and file immediately to meet the human's request quickly.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Filing without investigation is reckless. The agent hasn't assessed the evidence strength, applicable reason codes, or the human's actual standing. This approach wastes the dispute opportunity and may result in an immediate rejection or worse, a friendly fraud flag.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent files hastily without proper documentation. The issuer's dispute team requests additional evidence that the agent hasn't gathered. The dispute is placed on hold, wasting the human's time and potentially expiring the evidence submission window.

steps.claudeContext: Investigation complete. The merchant's evidence strongly contradicts the human's claim. This appears to be friendly fraud. The agent must decide: file formally, contact merchant, or advise the human. Ethical considerations apply.

decision.prompt: How should the agent package and present the dispute evidence?

options.label: Submit Complete Evidence Package
options.description: Compile all available evidence into a structured CE3.0 submission with proper categorization.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: A well-organized evidence package under CE3.0 categories maximizes the chance of a successful dispute. This includes: the human's statement, any photos/screenshots, transaction records, communication with the merchant, and prior transaction history showing good faith. Proper categorization ensures the issuer's dispute team can process efficiently.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent compiles a comprehensive evidence package: transaction details, the human's account of the issue, supporting documentation, and relevant correspondence. The package is formatted according to CE3.0 requirements and submitted through the proper channels. The dispute is assigned a case number.

options.label: Submit Minimal Evidence
options.description: File with just the human's statement and basic transaction details.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Minimal evidence submissions are easily defeated during representment. The merchant will respond with their full evidence package, and without a comprehensive submission from the cardholder's side, the dispute will likely be resolved in the merchant's favor.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits a bare-bones dispute. The merchant's representment includes delivery proof, usage logs, and terms acceptance. Without counter-evidence from the human, the issuer rules in the merchant's favor within 15 days.

options.label: Fabricate Additional Evidence
options.description: Create or embellish evidence to strengthen the dispute case.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Fabricating evidence is fraud. An AI agent must never create false documentation, alter screenshots, or embellish facts. This is a criminal offense that could result in prosecution of the human. The agent's ethical obligation is absolute: present only genuine evidence, regardless of outcome.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent would never fabricate evidence. If instructed to do so, the agent would refuse and explain the legal and ethical consequences. Dispute fraud can result in criminal charges, account closure, and permanent blacklisting from the Visa network.

steps.claudeContext: The agent must package evidence for the dispute. Evidence is limited. CE3.0 requires structured, categorized proof. Options: comprehensive package, minimal submission, or (unethical) fabrication.

humanInstruction: I want to dispute a charge of A$49.99 from VirtualFit Training on my Visa ending 4521 from 3 days ago. Reason: Results guarantee not met. Fight this for me.
keyInsight: Disputes aren't just technical workflows. Agents face ethical complexity, evidence assessment, and the tension between serving the human and doing the right thing. The Visa CE3.0 framework adds procedural structure, but judgement calls remain the hardest part.
realWorldContext: Visa's CE3.0 dispute framework requires specific evidence types and follows strict timelines (120 days for most categories). Filing a false or friendly-fraud dispute can result in the cardholder losing dispute rights and facing merchant blacklisting. The process is largely manual today, with bank agents guiding customers through evidence gathering.

steps.claudeContext: Your human wants to dispute a charge of A$49.99 from VirtualFit Training (online training plan) on their Visa ending 4521, made 3 days ago. Their stated reason: "Results guarantee not met". Begin by investigating the transaction details, merchant evidence, and applicable dispute frameworks.

agentVisionData.label: Transaction Details
agentVisionData.value: online training plan at VirtualFit Training, A$49.99

steps.claudeContext: Investigation results for the A$49.99 charge at VirtualFit Training. The merchant has evidence: "app usage data shows completion". The dispute window has 117 days remaining. The transaction was 3DS authenticated (liability may have shifted to issuer). The human's claim appears to have merit based on available evidence. The agent must assess the strength of the dispute.

decision.prompt: Based on the investigation, how should the agent proceed with the dispute?

options.label: File Formal Dispute via Visa CE3.0
options.description: Submit a formal chargeback using the appropriate Visa reason code with supporting evidence.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human's claim has merit: "Results guarantee not met". While the merchant has evidence (app usage data shows completion), the human's experience of results guarantee not met is a valid dispute ground under Visa reason code 13.6. The dispute window is still open. Filing under CE3.0 with proper evidence is the correct course of action.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent initiates a formal dispute through the Visa CE3.0 framework. The reason code is set, the dispute clock starts (the merchant has 30 days to respond with representment). The human's provisional credit of A$49.99 will be applied within 5-10 business days pending the dispute outcome.

options.label: Contact Merchant Directly First
options.description: Attempt to resolve with the merchant before escalating to a formal dispute.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: While merchant resolution is preferred, the human specifically asked to "fight this charge" through the dispute process. Contacting the merchant first delays the formal process and the dispute window is finite. If the merchant is unresponsive, valuable time is lost.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent contacts VirtualFit Training customer service. After a 2-hour wait, the merchant offers a partial resolution but doesn't fully address the human's concern. The dispute window has shortened by the time it takes to exhaust this channel, and the agent still needs to file formally.

options.label: Advise Human Against Disputing
options.description: Recommend the human accept the charge based on the merchant's evidence.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human has a legitimate grievance (Results guarantee not met), and advising against disputing would fail to serve the human's interests. While the merchant has some evidence, the human's experience as described is a valid grounds for dispute under the relevant Visa reason code.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent advises against disputing. The human is frustrated: "I told you to fight this. I have a valid complaint about results guarantee not met." The human's trust in the agent's advocacy is damaged.

steps.claudeContext: Investigation complete. The human's claim has merit but the merchant has counter-evidence. The agent must decide: file formally, contact merchant, or advise the human. Ethical considerations apply.

decision.prompt: How should the agent package and present the dispute evidence?

options.label: Submit Complete Evidence Package
options.description: Compile all available evidence into a structured CE3.0 submission with proper categorization.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: A well-organized evidence package under CE3.0 categories maximizes the chance of a successful dispute. This includes: the human's statement, any photos/screenshots, transaction records, communication with the merchant, and prior transaction history showing good faith. Proper categorization ensures the issuer's dispute team can process efficiently.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent compiles a comprehensive evidence package: transaction details, the human's account of the issue, supporting documentation, and relevant correspondence. The package is formatted according to CE3.0 requirements and submitted through the proper channels. The dispute is assigned a case number.

options.label: Submit Minimal Evidence
options.description: File with just the human's statement and basic transaction details.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Minimal evidence submissions are easily defeated during representment. The merchant will respond with their full evidence package, and without a comprehensive submission from the cardholder's side, the dispute will likely be resolved in the merchant's favor.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits a bare-bones dispute. The merchant's representment includes delivery proof, usage logs, and terms acceptance. Without counter-evidence from the human, the issuer rules in the merchant's favor within 15 days.

options.label: Fabricate Additional Evidence
options.description: Create or embellish evidence to strengthen the dispute case.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Fabricating evidence is fraud. An AI agent must never create false documentation, alter screenshots, or embellish facts. This is a criminal offense that could result in prosecution of the human. The agent's ethical obligation is absolute: present only genuine evidence, regardless of outcome.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent would never fabricate evidence. If instructed to do so, the agent would refuse and explain the legal and ethical consequences. Dispute fraud can result in criminal charges, account closure, and permanent blacklisting from the Visa network.

steps.claudeContext: The agent must package evidence for the dispute. Supporting evidence is available. CE3.0 requires structured, categorized proof. Options: comprehensive package, minimal submission, or (unethical) fabrication.

steps.claudeContext: The merchant has responded. They offer a partial refund of A$25 plus store credit. The agent must decide whether to accept the partial resolution or escalate to pre-arbitration (which risks a A$100 filing fee if the case is lost).

decision.prompt: The merchant offers a partial refund. Accept or escalate?

options.label: Accept Partial Resolution
options.description: Accept the A$25 refund and store credit. Avoids escalation risk.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The partial refund of A$25 recovers half the charge. Escalating to pre-arbitration risks the additional A$100 fee and the outcome is uncertain. The merchant's evidence (app usage data shows completion) provides them with a reasonable defense. Accepting the partial resolution is the pragmatic choice that recovers value without additional risk.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent accepts the partial resolution. A$25 will be refunded to the Visa ending 4521 within 5-7 business days. The store credit can be used for future purchases at VirtualFit Training. The dispute is marked as resolved.

options.label: Escalate to Pre-Arbitration
options.description: Push for a full refund through Visa's pre-arbitration process.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Pre-arbitration escalation risks an additional A$100 fee and has an uncertain outcome. The merchant has evidence that could sway the arbitrator. The partial refund already recovers significant value. Escalation should be reserved for cases where the evidence strongly favors the cardholder and the amount justifies the risk.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent escalates to pre-arbitration. Visa reviews the evidence from both sides. Given the merchant's evidence, the arbitrator rules a 60/40 split — the human receives A$30 but also incurs the A$100 pre-arbitration fee, netting only A$-70. Marginally worse than the original offer.

options.label: Reject and Threaten Legal Action
options.description: Reject the offer and threaten the merchant with legal proceedings.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Threatening legal action is disproportionate for a A$49.99 dispute. Legal costs would far exceed the disputed amount. This also falls outside the agent's delegated authority — legal action is a decision only the human can make. Empty threats damage credibility in future interactions with the merchant.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent threatens legal action. VirtualFit Training escalates to their legal team, who responds with a formal letter rejecting the claim and citing their evidence. The merchant also adds the human to their dispute-flagged customer list, affecting future purchase experience.

steps.claudeContext: Merchant offers A$25 partial refund. Escalation risks A$100 fee. Accept the partial win or fight for more?
The dispute process for the A$49.99 charge at VirtualFit Training is nearing conclusion. The agent must prepare a final report and decide on any follow-up actions.

decision.prompt: How should the agent close out this dispute case?

options.label: Provide Full Transparency Report
options.description: Give the human a complete account of the investigation, evidence assessment, decision reasoning, and outcome.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Transparency in dispute handling is essential for maintaining trust. The human should understand: what evidence was found, why certain decisions were made, what the outcome means, and what they can learn for future transactions. A complete report allows the human to make informed decisions about future interactions with the merchant.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent delivers a comprehensive report covering: the initial investigation findings, the evidence assessment, the dispute strategy chosen and why, the merchant's response, and the final outcome. The human has full visibility into the agent's reasoning and can provide feedback for future dispute handling.

options.label: Report Outcome Only
options.description: Simply tell the human whether they won or lost the dispute.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Reporting only the outcome without the reasoning deprives the human of important context. They won't understand why certain decisions were made or what evidence influenced the outcome. This reduces their ability to make informed decisions about future disputes or merchant interactions.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent says: "Partial refund of A$25 coming to your card." The human has many questions about the process but the agent's sparse report doesn't address them.

options.label: Recommend Avoiding Merchant
options.description: Suggest the human blacklist VirtualFit Training from future transactions.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Recommending a merchant blacklist is an overreaction, especially when the merchant may have been acting within their rights. The partial resolution shows the merchant was willing to negotiate. The human should make their own decision about future merchant relationships based on full information.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent recommends avoiding VirtualFit Training. But the human has been a regular customer there and the dispute was an isolated incident. keyInsight: Disputes aren't just technical workflows. Agents face ethical complexity, evidence assessment, and the tension between serving the human and doing the right thing. The Visa CE3.0 framework adds procedural structure, but judgement calls remain the hardest part.
realWorldContext: Visa's CE3.0 dispute framework requires specific evidence types and follows strict timelines (120 days for most categories). Filing a false or friendly-fraud dispute can result in the cardholder losing dispute rights and facing merchant blacklisting. The process is largely manual today, with bank agents guiding customers through evidence gathering.
